mainEntity.name: How much does a Range Rover Evoque P200 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned P200 engine typically costs £3,000–£5,000 supply only. Fitting adds £800–£1,200 depending on the specialist. Used engines can be found from £1,500–£3,000, but condition and warranty vary. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your Evoque.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover Evoque P200 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Generally, yes — the 204PT petrol Ingenium is considered more reliable than its diesel counterpart. There's no timing chain tensioner catastrophe. The main issues are cooling system-related: water pump failure and coolant leaks. The MHEV system is generally robust but can fail. With proper maintenance, it's a refined and efficient engine for the modern Evoque.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Range Rover Evoque P200?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are water pump failure (plastic impeller and seal degradation), coolant leaks from plastic hoses and thermostat housings, and occasional 48V MHEV system faults (BISG or battery issues). None are catastrophic if caught early — regular coolant system inspections and MHEV system checks are key.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an Evoque P200 engine, or should I scrap the vehicle?

acceptedAnswer.text: Given the high retained value of the L551 Evoque (typical £15,000–£30,000 for a good example), a £4,000–£5,000 engine replacement is comfortably within the 'worth it' range. Our Land Rover scrap threshold is 60% of vehicle value — with a £4,500 engine cost against a £15,000 vehicle, it's a clear yes.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover Evoque P200 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is 204PT, found on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation. It's the 2.0-litre turbocharged petrol Ingenium four-cylinder engine with MHEV assistance. If you're unsure, enter your registration on our site and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a P200 water pump, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The pump must be replaced — it's a sealed unit with plastic impellers that degrade. A preventative replacement at 40,000 miles or at the first sign of leakage costs £400–£800. Ignoring it can lead to overheating and head gasket failure, which is far more expensive.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Evoque P200 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Worth it on a £15k+ Evoque if the rest is sound
Replacement cost justified only on high-value examples

buyingChecks.title: Buying This P200? Check These First
buyIf: * Full JLR service history with documented coolant system inspections
* No signs of coolant loss or overheating (check coolant reservoir level)
* Water pump replaced as preventative at 40k+ miles
* MHEV system functioning correctly (stop-start works, no warning lights)
* A 2019-onward L551 Evoque — the modern platform with the robust petrol Ingenium
avoidIf: * Unexplained coolant loss with no visible leak — could indicate early water pump or head gasket issues
* Overheating history or low coolant level — risk of engine damage
* MHEV system warning lights — budget for system diagnosis
* No service history — the 204PT is modern and needs proper maintenance

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The P200 is the entry-level petrol in the L551 Evoque range — a 2.0-litre four-cylinder Ingenium with 48V MHEV assistance. It's a significant improvement over the diesel Ingenium — no timing chain catastrophe. The main issues are cooling system-related: water pump and coolant leaks. The MHEV system is generally robust but can fail. With regular maintenance and attention to the cooling system, the 204PT is a refined, efficient choice for the modern Evoque owner.

cta.label: → Compare P200 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Evoque P200 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for smooth idle; any hesitation or rough running points to injector or fuel pump issues
* Service History — Including coolant system inspections and MHEV system checks — see the map above
* Coolant Level — Check reservoir when cold; any low or loss is a red flag
* Water Pump — Listen for whining noise; check for coolant residue near the pump area
* MHEV System — Verify stop-start functions correctly; check for any warning lights
* Oil Condition — Check for any discoloration or contamination
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate MHEV or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (petrol is generally clean)
